programs.name: SoonerCare
programs.what_it_does: SoonerCare is Oklahoma's Medicaid program providing health care coverage. It serves seniors, disabled individuals, children, pregnant women, and adults with children.[7]
programs.who_its_for: People older than 65, those with blindness or disabilities, low-income children under 19, pregnant women, and adults with children under 18.[7]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by eligibility; covers medical services for qualified low-income individuals.

programs.name: ADvantage Waiver Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides home and community-based services (HCBS) such as personal care assistance, assisted living, adult day health care, home modifications, and meals for elderly or disabled individuals at risk of nursing home placement. Services allow participants to live in their own home, a family member's home, or approved assisted living rather than institutional settings.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Oklahoma residents aged 65+ or disabled adults aged 21-64 at risk of nursing home placement; excludes those with developmental disabilities involving cognitive or intellectual impairment.[2]
programs.whats_unique: Oklahoma's state-specific Medicaid waiver program tailored to prevent institutionalization with customizable HCBS, including transition services from nursing homes; not a standard federal program but a state-administered waiver.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by need; includes personal care hours, home modifications (e.g., ramps, grab bars), meals, and assistive technology; no fixed dollar amount as services are in-kind.[2]

programs.name: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.what_it_does: No specific Oklahoma program details found in search results.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program
programs.what_it_does: Oklahoma's Medicare Savings Program (includes QMB, SLMB, QI) helps pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and cost-sharing. It provides low-income subsidy or 'Extra Help' for prescription drugs.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Covers Medicare Part A/B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments; Extra Help for Part D.

programs.name: SNAP
programs.what_it_does: The federal Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) provides a monthly benefit to buy food. It is administered through Oklahoma DHS.[7]
programs.benefit_value: Monthly EBT benefits for eligible food purchases.

programs.name: LIHEAP
programs.what_it_does: No specific Oklahoma details found in search results.

programs.name: Weatherization
programs.what_it_does: No specific Oklahoma senior program details found in search results.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Counseling Program (SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: SHIP provides free counseling on Medicare options, including Medicare Advantage, Part D, and supplemental plans. It is part of the Medicare Assistance Program funded by ACL grants.[2]

programs.name: Home Delivered Meals (under Title III)
programs.what_it_does: Home Delivered Meals provide nutritious meals to homebound seniors meeting one-third of daily nutritional requirements. Meals are planned by registered dietitians.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 60+, caregivers, individuals with disabilities, low-income persons.[4]

programs.name: Respite Voucher Program
programs.what_it_does: The Respite Voucher Program funds vouchers for caregivers to pay others for temporary care, providing breaks from caregiving duties. It supports family caregivers.[7][4]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of older adults or those with disabilities.[7]
programs.benefit_value: Voucher payments for respite care services.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: No specific Oklahoma details found in search results.

programs.name: Legal aid for seniors
programs.what_it_does: No specific Oklahoma program name or details found in search results.

programs.name: Long-term care ombudsman

programs.name: Senior Farmers’ Market Nutrition Program (SFMNP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers financial support in the form of vouchers for low-income seniors to purchase fresh produce from local farmers’ markets. Promotes nutrition and access to healthy foods through state-coordinated incentives.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors in Oklahoma, typically those eligible for similar federal nutrition programs.[1]
programs.whats_unique: Oklahoma's state-implemented version provides targeted financial assistance for fresh produce at local markets, supplementing federal efforts with state-specific administration and focus.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Financial vouchers for fresh produce purchases; exact amount varies by program funding and eligibility (typically $20-$50 annually per participant based on similar state programs).[1]
